EXCELLENCE GUIDED BY PURPOSE
ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS

TO BE THE PREMIER
FINANCIAL SERVICES
GROUP OF AFRICAN
ORIGIN.
TO BUILD A SUPPORTIVE ECOSYSTEM
ROOTED IN AFRICA, THAT CONNECTS
PEOPLE, CAPITAL AND MARKETS.
OUR VISION
EXECUTION
PROFESSIONALISM
INNOVATION
CUSTOMER FOCUS
OUR MISSION
OUR CORE VALUES

We are doing this by democratising access to comprehensive financial services beyond just a bank account, promoting sustainable economic growth by unlocking transformative capital, export and diaspora flows,  and taking climate action through lending, investment and operational practices.
AT FCMB,
OUR PURPOSE
IS TO FOSTER
INCLUSIVE AND
SUSTAINABLE
GROWTH IN THE
COMMUNITIES
WE SERVE.
EXCELLENCE DRIVEN BY PURPOSE 3

FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2024

Introduction

The origins of FCMB Group Plc date back to 1977 with the founding of City
Securities Limited (CSL) - a combined stockbroking and issuing house business) established by Otunba Michael
Olasubomi Balogun who transcended to eternal glory on the 18th day of May
2023.
More information can be found at:
The legacy of the founder of FCMB Group lives on through the institutions he built and inspired, the bankers and leaders he trained, the lives he touched and the values he exemplified. These serve not only as a foundation for which the Group and its stakeholders are eternally grateful, but as also a beacon of hope and a guiding light for every member of FCMB Group. Over the next few pages, we share a few excerpts from his messages in our prior annual reports.
The success of CSL in the Nigerian capital markets, prompted Otunba Balogun to establish First City
Merchant Bank Limited as the 1st independent merchant bank founded by a Nigerian. The bank has since expanded and evolved into a financial services group, with operating companies divided along four business groups: The Banking Group (First City Monument Bank
Limited ('The Bank'), FCMB Bank UK Limited and FCMB
Microfinance Limited); Consumer Finance (Credit Direct
Finance Company Limited); Investment Banking (FCMB
Capital Markets Limited and CSL Stockbrokers Limited);
and Investment Management (FCMB Pensions Limited,
FCMB Asset Management Limited and FCMB Trustees
Limited). Listed on the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) with the ticker symbol 'FCMB', FCMB Group Plc has
39.6 billion ordinary shares held by over 555,000 shareholders.

Limited is established

EXCELLENCE DRIVEN BY PURPOSE

Mr. Oladipupo Jadesimi
Chairman, FCMB Group Plc
FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2024

I would like to begin by expressing my sincere gratitude for your unwavering commitment and the confidence you have placed in FCMB Group Plc. Your steadfast support, particularly through your participation in our capital raise via the public offer, has been instrumental in strengthening our foundation for sustainable growth. Together, we have not only reinforced the Group's financial position but also advanced our vision of creating long-term value. Your trust and partnership continue to play a vital role in our success story, and we remain committed to delivering even greater returns in the years ahead.
In response, the CBN maintained its tight monetary policy stance, raising the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) six times to
27.5 percent by December 2024. On the Fiscal side, the government proposed transformative fiscal reforms to enhance Nigeria's business climate, streamline tax administration, and boost revenue generation to foster economic growth. Overall, Nigeria's GDP grew 3.5 percent in 2024, compared to 2.7% in 2023, driven primarily by growth in the services sector and a marked increase in the trade balance.
Low performance in agriculture and manufacturing remained a concern, given their crucial role in food security, job creation, and economic diversification.
The year 2024 presented a challenging macroeconomic environment, marked by global uncertainties and domestic economic adjustments. Domestically, Nigeria's economy navigated a mix of opportunities and challenges shaped by fluctuating oil prices, depreciating NGN, and rising fiscal deficits. Bold policy moves such as the petrol subsidy removal and foreign exchange market reforms aimed at stabilizing the economy by improving the government's fiscal position and enabling more foreign capital inflows, presented significant social and political trade-offs.
Escalating inflation, which reached a 26-year high of 34.6 percent in November 2024, driven by surging food and energy prices, placed a heavy burden on households and businesses.
Despite these headwinds, FCMB Group Plc remained resilient, capitalizing on the strength of its unique Group structure and the collective efforts of its operating companies which gives me the great pleasure of reporting that the company recorded a full year profit before tax of N111.9 billion for the 2024 Financial Year, a 7.1% growth from the previous year, 2023. This strategic advantage enabled us to navigate complexities effectively, drive operational efficiencies, and deliver a strong financial performance.
Excellence guided by Purpose
Excellence is the foundation upon which our institution was built and the driving force behind everything we do. Our journey has been defined by a relentless pursuit of excellence, grounded in strong values, visionary leadership, and an unwavering commitment to delivering sustainable value to all stakeholders. From inception, we have embraced our values of Execution, Professionalism, Innovation, and
Customer Focus, ensuring that our operations, products, and services consistently uphold the highest standards. However, our definition of excellence extends beyond financial performance; it is deeply intertwined with our core purpose - to foster inclusive and sustainable growth in the communities we serve. This purpose drives us to create solutions that empower individuals, businesses, and economies, ensuring that prosperity is not just for a few, but for the many. It inspires our investments in technology, financial inclusion, and impact-driven initiatives that improve lives and create lasting change. As we navigate an evolving economic landscape, we remain resolute in our mission, leveraging our
Group structure and collective strengths to build a future where excellence is not only measured by our achievements but by the positive and sustainable impact we create. This commitment will continue to shape our strategic direction, guiding our decisions and reinforcing our dedication to fostering inclusive and sustainable growth. Going forward, we will place deliberate focus on embedding excellence across every facet of our business operations - strengthening governance, enhancing customer experience, driving innovation, and improving operational efficiency.
Distinguished ladies and gentlemen, fellow shareholders, it is both a pleasure and an honor to welcome you to the 12th Annual General
Meeting of FCMB Group Plc and to present the Annual Report and
Accounts for the financial year ended 31 December 2024.
Chairman's Statement

EXCELLENCE DRIVEN BY PURPOSE

Our Board's Focus
The Board of the Group has the responsibility for monitoring the activities of all group companies under its ownership, which include First City Monument Bank (FCMB) Limited,
FCMB Capital Markets Limited, CSL Stockbrokers Limited,
FCMB Trustees Limited, FCMB Microfinance Bank Limited,
FCMB Pensions Limited and Credit Direct Finance Company
Limited. Two of our companies also monitor subsidiaries that they own: FCMB Limited monitors and own FCMB (UK)
Limited, and CSL Stockbrokers Limited monitors and own
FCMB Asset Management Ltd. The Group remains committed to the implementation of the Corporate Governance standards and regulations of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), the  and the Securities and
Exchange Commission and the United Kingdom's Prudential
Regulatory Authority and Financial Conduct Authority.
As at 31 December 2024, the Board led by myself as NonExecutive Chairman, was composed of nine other Directors (Six Non-Executive Directors and three Executive Directors), in line with international best practice that requires the number of Non-Executive Directors to be more than the
Executive Directors. Mr. Ladi Balogun (as the Group Chief
Executive), Mr. Gbolahan Joshua (as the Chief Operating
Officer) and Mr. Olufemi Badeji (as Executive Director:
Coverage and Investment Banking) made up the three
Executive Directors, while the Non-Executive Directors (besides myself) comprised Alhaji Mustapha Damcida, Mrs.
Olapeju Sofowora, Professor Oluwatoyin Ashiru OON, Dr.
(Engr) Gregory O. Ero., Mrs. 'Tokunboh Ishmael and Ms.
Muibat Ijaiya. The Board met five times during 2024 with a
94% attendance rate. The Board was supported by the
Statutory Audit Committee and four Board Committees that reported to it, namely the Board Investment Committee, the
Board Risk Management Committee, the Board Audit
Committee and the Board Governance and Remuneration
Committee. The Board Investment Committee which consisted of Mrs. 'Tokunboh Ishmael (Chairperson), Ms. Muibat
Ijaiya, Mrs. Olapeju Sofowora, Mr. Ladi Balogun, Mr. Femi
Badeji and Mr. Gbolahan Joshua met two times in 2024 with an average attendance rate of 92%. The Board Risk
Management Committee which consisted of Ms. Muibat
Ijaiya (Chairperson), Mrs. Olapeju Sofowora, Mrs. 'Tokunboh
Ishmael, Mr. Ladi Balogun and Mr. Gbolahan met four times in
2024 with an average attendance rate of 100%. The Board
Audit Committee which was made up of only Non-Executive
Directors, consisted of Mrs. Olapeju Sofowora (Chairperson),
Dr. (Engr.) Gregory O. Ero, Mrs. 'Tokunboh Ishmael and Ms.
Muibat Ijaiya met five times in 2024 with an average of 85% attendance rate. The Board Governance and Remuneration
Committee, which was made up of only Non-Executive
Directors, consisted of the following as members during the year: Professor Oluwatoyin Ashiru OON (Chairman), Alhaji
Mustapha Damcida, Mrs. Olapeju Sofowora and Mrs.
'Tokunboh Ishmael. The committee met four times during the year, with an average attendance rate of 100%.
The Board of Directors remains committed in its commitment to upholding the Group's corporate culture, strategic vision, and long-term objectives. We recognize that strong governance, sound leadership, and a clearly defined strategy are critical to driving sustainable growth and delivering value to all stakeholders. Our Board comprises a diverse group of seasoned professionals, each bringing a wealth of expertise, independence, and deep industry experience necessary to guide FCMB Group towards its ambition of becoming one of the leading financial services institutions of African origin.
The Directors' profiles, detailed in the corporate governance section of this report, highlight the breadth of their business acumen and the strategic oversight they provide in shaping the Group's future. With a shared commitment to excellence, innovation, and sustainable impact, the Board continues to provide the necessary stewardship and oversight to strengthen our competitive position, foster inclusive growth, and create long-term value for our shareholders, customers, employees, and the broader economy.
Board Composition and Committees
Chairman's Statement
As we navigate an evolving economic landscape, we remain resolute in our mission, leveraging our Group structure and collective strengths to build a future where excellence is not only measured by our achievements but by the positive and sustainable impact we create.

The Statutory Audit Committee which consisted of
Evangelist Akinola Soares (Chairman), Alhaji S. B. Daranijo,
Mr. Hakeem Batula, Professor Oluwatoyin Ashiru OON, and
Mrs. Olapeju Sofowora met four times, with a 100% attendance rate. These committees enabled the Board of FCMB
Group Plc to monitor and supervise the implementation of business plans of each company in the Group on a regular and consistent basis.
To our valued customers, your loyalty and trust in the FCMB brand continues to inspire us, and we are deeply grateful. I also acknowledge our Board and Management for their visionary leadership, which has kept us steadfast in our journey towards becoming a premier financial services group of African origin.
Earnings and Dividend Per Share Information
I extend my heartfelt appreciation to all who played a role in our financial and non-financial achievements through the year. The dedication and commitment of our staff have been key to delivering and sustaining our performance, and I sincerely thank all of you.
This commitment is grounded on the deliberate consideration of facilitating sustainable business growth and capital requirements, with the overarching goal of optimizing longterm value for our shareholders.
Lastly, to our esteemed shareholders, your unwavering trust and support remain the cornerstone of our success. The
Board and Management remain fully committed to delivering sustainable growth and enhancing long-term shareholder value.
For the full year ended 31 December 2024, the Group declared a profit after tax of N73.3 billion, representing an
Earnings Per Share of N2.46. More details on the performance will be provided by the Group Chief Executive.
Accordingly, on behalf of the Board, I am pleased to inform our shareholders that the Group has resolved to pay a final dividend of 55 kobo per share subject to shareholder approval at the Annual General Meeting. The Board of
Directors at FCMB Group Plc remain committed to delivering sustainable returns to shareholders.
Acknowledgments
A special note of appreciation goes to our regulators, including the Central Bank of Nigeria, the Nigerian Exchange
Group, and the  , for their continuous support and guidance.
Mr. Oladipupo Jadesimi
Chairman

FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2023
Mr. Ladi Balogun
Group CEO, FCMB Group Plc
FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2024

Macroeconomic and Operating Environment
The global economy remained resilient through 2024, avoiding a widespread economic contraction despite enduring years of interconnected headwinds and risks including supply chain disruptions, geopolitical conflicts, and the most prolonged inflation-driven monetary tightening in recent history. Given this context, the global economy in 2024 performed better overall than many had feared around this time last year. The rebound in the global economy could be attributed to the pick-up of economic activities in advanced and emerging economies. In 2024, global headline inflation continued to ease gradually, falling from 5.6 per cent in 2023 to an estimated 4.0 per cent in
2024, creating space for central banks to begin lowering interest rates, easing financial conditions and providing some welcome relief to households, businesses, and even countries struggling with debt repayments. Consequently, the IMF has projected a global growth rate of 3.2 per cent for 2024.
024 presented numerous challenges alongside opportunities, with significant global and domestic economic developments influencing the economic and business landscape including the financial services sector. Given this backdrop, it is important that we assess the economic and financial landscape in which we operated over the past fiscal year.
Domestically, the Nigerian economy recorded mixed performances across different socioeconomic indicators during the year. The implementation of significant reforms to stabilize the economy, resulted in modest growth, improving fiscal health, and foreign exchange reserves.
While these measures were essential to avert a fiscal crisis and ensure a stronger development path for the country, they also triggered short-term pressures on households and businesses. The aftermaths of fuel subsidy removal, exchange rate depreciation, and other major reforms continued to impact operating costs and prices of goods and services. As a result, inflation surged as high as 34.8% in
December 2024 with food inflation being the dominant driver of inflationary pressures. In terms of  the exchange rate, price discovery remained turbulent. Opening the year at N907/US$, the NGN in the official Investors & Exporters (I&E) Window weakened to as low as N1,665/US$ in midFebruary. It, however, appreciated to N1,072/US$ in midApril closing the year at N1535/US$ representing a 40.9% depreciation for 2024.
The US economy remained on a solid growth trajectory supported by healthy employment and income growth, resilient consumer spending, and rising productivity that is helping tame inflationary pressures.  Similarly, in the
Eurozone, cautious optimism seen at the start of 2024 partly materialized. The EU economy started growing in the first quarter of this year, and growth continued at a steady but subdued pace through the second and third quarters, with inflationary pressures easing. Overall, Real GDP growth for the Eurozone is expected to be 0.8% in 2024. Among emerging economies, China's recovery gathered strength as it announced further stimulus measures. Cross-border trade picked up pace, driven primarily by a surge in exports growth.  While recent policy easing measures have provided moderate support, subdued household and business confidence, along with headwinds in the property sector continued weighing on growth. China's growth is estimated at 4.9 percent in 2024.
Nigeria's current account surged to a record-high 11.46% of
GDP in H1 2024, driven by a trade surplus and increased remittance inflows. Trade performance benefited from elevated oil prices, which helped offset the impact of weaker oil production during the period. The positive current account balance contributed to the growth of FX reserves and improved FX liquidity in the market. Capital importation was valued at $7.23 billion between January and September
2024, a 157% growth when compared to $2.82 billion recorded in the corresponding period of 2023. Consequently, the country's foreign reserves closed at US$40.9 billion, up by US$8.0 billion from US$32.9 billion in 2023.
Group Chief Executive's Report
Distinguished Shareholders, it gives me great pleasure to welcome you to the 12th Annual General Meeting of FCMB Group Plc. I am honoured to present a summary of our business performance for the year 2024.

.
In March 2024, the Central Bank of Nigeria announced a significant increase in paid-up capital requirements (share capital plus share premium) for commercial, merchant and non-interest banks in line with its ambition to enhance the resilience, solvency and capacity of the Banking Sector to continue to support the growth of the Nigerian economy. To meet the minimum capital requirement, Banks have three ways to comply - through equity injections, M&A and downgrading their license authorisation. In terms of financial soundness indicators, the banking system capital adequacy ratio (CAR) stood at 14.01 per cent compared to 11.2 per cent recorded in June 2023 while industry asset quality stood at
4.58 per cent below the prudential benchmark of 5.0 per cent.
The Nigerian pension industry continued to record steady growth despite the prevalent macroeconomic challenges.
The industry AUM recorded a 22.65 per cent increase to
N22.5 trillion in December 2024 compared with N18.4 trillion in December 2023. Similarly, the number of RSA registrations also increased to 10.58 million representing a 3.84% increase, equivalent to 390,899 new registrants within the year. In terms of retirement savings account movement, a total of N391.6 billion transferred industry-wide, up from
N356.3 billion recorded for 9M 2023 while total industry
RSA transfers declined to 67,146 as at Q3 2024 from 78,336 in the same period prior year.
The dynamic policy environment led to mixed sentiments for investors and across the capital markets segment as a whole. The removal of subsidies and unification of the foreign exchange market fueled a bullish equity market earlier in the year. However, the elevated interest rate environment following increases in the MPR dampened the equity market's trajectory. Despite this, as of the end of
December, the NGX-ASI closed at 102,926.4, a growth from
74,773.7 at the start of the year, signaling a remarkable turnaround in the Nigerian Stock Market.
Group Chief Executive's Report
Despite the challenging business landscape, our performance in 2024 was sustained by the commitment and professionalism of our talented staff and the resilience exhibited by each of our operating companies.

Overall Group Performance
Despite the challenging business landscape, our performance in 2024 was sustained by the commitment and professionalism of our talented staff and the resilience exhibited by each of our operating companies. The strength of our ecosystem, built over time, enabled us to navigate challenges and sustain growth, albeit modestly.
A review of our FY 2024 results highlights a positive trend across key financial indicators. FCMB Group's pre-tax profits were up 7.1% to N111.9 billion from N104.4 billion recorded in
December 2023. With the addition of 19.8 billion shares in
December 2024 from the public offer, this translates to a
Return on Average Equity (ROAE) of 12.8% and Earnings per
Share (EPS) of N2.46 kobo for the year. In terms of our balance sheet position, the Group's loan book grew by
688.2bn
4.4tn
7.1tn

FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2024

Our total number of customers crossed 14 million, with over
3.4 million active, and our agent network grew to over
435,000. Assets Under Management stood at N1.37 trillion (of which 60% was retail). Lending to SME's, Agriculture and women owned businesses exceeded N425 billion, N271 billion and N30 billion, growing year on year by 31%, 33% and 68% respectively. All of the above demonstrate our commitment to our purpose of fostering inclusive growth.
Our Investment Management businesses (FCMB Pensions
Limited, FCMB Asset Management Limited and FCMB
Trustees Limited) collectively grew Assets Under Management by 35.0 percent to N1.37 trillion at the end of the year.
For the period, our Pensions business grew Retirement
Savings Accounts (RSAs)  by 3 percent from the preceding year despite a fiercely competitive 'Transfer Window' under the RSA transfer programme. It also grew Assets under
Management (AUM) by 20.2% to close at N947.5 billion while recording 13 percent growth in pre-tax profits.
Business Groups' Performance
Our Investment Banking division (CSL Stockbrokers Limited and FCMB Capital Markets Limited) recorded a combined pre-tax profit of N1.8 billion for the period under review, down 35 percent from 2023. FCMB Capital Markets Limited recorded a pre-tax profit growth of 62.2 percent driven by increased equity capital market activities.
Our Consumer Finance business (Credit Direct Finance
Company Limited) demonstrated resilience during the period, delivering an 83.5 percent growth in pre-tax profits to N12.3 billion for the year 2024. This was driven by an increase in loan disbursement from N60.5 billion in 2023 to
N100.3 billion in 2024 while recording a 50-basis point yearon-year reduction in Non-Performing Loan Ratio.
28.0% from N1.84 trillion in December 2023 to N2.36 trillion in December 2024, while customer deposits increased by
39.4% to N4.30 trillion from N3.08 trillion in December 2023.
Total assets grew by 59.5% Year-on-Year (YoY) to N7.05 trillion while shareholders' funds grew by 49.3% YoY to
N688.2 billion in December 2024, driven by profit growth and the additional capital raised.
The Banking Group (First City Monument Bank Limited and
FCMB Bank UK Limited) recorded 7.7 percent  decline in pre-tax profits to N77.5 billion for the year 2024 despite a
53.2 percent growth in gross earnings. The business recorded a 73.0 percent growth in interest income driven by growth in loans and advances to customers and a 5.4 percent growth in non-interest income. Net fees and commissions grew by 29.7 percent while FX gains declined by 62.5 percent. This decline was however offset  by a 667.2 percent growth in net-trading income. Our UK franchise (FCMB Bank UK Limited) recorded a 38.2 percent  growth in pre-tax profits in 2024.
Following the Central Bank of Nigeria's announcement on the review of the minimum capital requirements for Banks, we successfully raised N140 billion via a public offer, exceeding the N200 billion required to retain our national license. This has laid a solid foundation for the subsequent phases in which we are seeking to raise an additional N240 billion to retain our international license. Consequently, we saw Capital Adequacy Ratio rise to 18 percent for the Group as at December 2024. We believe the proceeds from our capital raise will unlock the next phase of FCMB Group's strategic growth and expansion.
Our investments in technology over the years have enabled us to build digital resilience by streamlining business processes, improving efficiency and service delivery to our customers as digital revenues exceeded N93.7 billion, a growth of 55% from 2023. The major contributor to this was digital lending, particularly from our consumer finance business. Going into 2025 and beyond, we expect more significant and diversified contributions from digitization with a focus on digital onboarding, payments, and artificial intelligence.
Our investment banking franchise also raised over N1.3 trillion (up by 47% from 2023) to support the growth of our clients and, consequently, the Nigerian economy.
Going into 2025 and beyond, we expect more significant and diversified contributions from digitization with a focus on digital onboarding, payments, and artificial intelligence.
" "
Group Chief Executive's Report

Outlook
Going into 2025, our focus remains on accelerating our digital transformation, reinforcing our culture of excellence and extending the power of the Group in building a supportive ecosystem in fulfilment of our purpose.
With the collective support of our ecosystem, including our people, investors, regulators, customers and partners, we will remain  committed to carrying forward the vision of our
Founder, building an institution, nation and continent in which future generations can take pride. It is both our responsibility and privilege to contribute to this transformative journey, driving sustainable growth and innovation across the continent.
The IMF has projected global growth at 3.3 percent both in
2025 and 2026, below the historical (2000-19) average of
3.7 percent as an upward revision in the United States offsets the downward revisions in other major economies.
Global headline inflation is expected to decline to 4.2 percent in 2025 and to 3.5 percent in 2026.
In closing, I would like to thank all our stakeholders for their unwavering support throughout the year.
Domestically, growth prospects are likely to be slightly elevated with the implementation of effective stabilization measures which are expected to enable broad-based sectoral growth in the short term. Key drivers, such as foreign exchange stability will sustain operations in the manufacturing sector, while power supply and fuel pricing stability are anticipated to reduce business disruptions thereby boosting overall economic performance. The effective coordination of fiscal and monetary policy measures will also be pivotal in curbing inflation. Real GDP growth is projected to rise to 5.5 percent in 2025.
Thank you very much for your attention. God bless you all.
Ladi Balogun
Group Chief Executive

Sustainability Report
BUILDING
ECOSYSTEMS,
EMPOWERING
COMMUNITIES.
We have adopted internationally recognised reporting frameworks, including the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and the United Nations Global Compact, to ensure alignment with global standards. This approach reflects our commitment to accurate, transparent, and continuous sustainability reporting. Additionally, we acknowledge the
Financial Reporting Council of Nigeria's leadership in establishing a roadmap for sustainability disclosures. In line with this, we have opted into the Voluntary Phase, allowing us to refine our reporting approach, enhance accuracy, and optimise our sustainability disclosure framework for longterm impact.
Recognizing this responsibility, the Group integrates sustainability principles and governance into its purpose, business strategy, and performance evaluation framework, ensuring that its goals and future practices align with longterm environmental, social, and economic impact. As a leading financial institution, we recognize our role in driving economic growth, fostering social progress, and championing environmental stewardship. Sustainability is at the core of our purpose, guiding our decisions across operations, products, and services.
Our ESG Approach
This report, themed "Building Ecosystems and Empowering
Communities," highlights our progress and achievements in embedding sustainability principles into our core business operations. It has been prepared in alignment with the Central
Bank of Nigeria's Nigerian Sustainable Banking Principles (NSBPs) and the Nigerian Exchange's Sustainability Disclosure Guidelines (SDG) to provide stakeholders with a transparent view of our sustainability performance and commitments.
We take great pride in ensuring that all our stakeholders - including our natural environment, communities, shareholders, and employees - benefit from how we conduct our business. We have developed a robust ESG framework based on direct engagement with key stakeholders to
FCMB Group acknowledges its role as a key player in Nigeria's financial ecosystem and the sector's influence in advancing the United Nations
Sustainable Development Goals and Global Climate Action. Through its operations and engagements across various economic sectors, FCMB
Group actively promotes the adoption of sustainable practices.
EXCELLENCE DRIVEN BY PURPOSE

Sustainability Report identify and address material issues affecting our business.
This framework enables us to strategically prioritise initiatives, ensuring that our efforts generate significant economic, social, and environmental impact where it matters most. Through a comprehensive assessment of key issues - evaluated based on their impact on stakeholders and our business - we identified our most critical sustainability priorities. This assessment involved analysing past Sustainability and Corporate Social Responsibility initiatives, which highlight the areas that business leadership and key stakeholders, guided by market-driven insights, have recognised as the most effective for sustainability action.
We remain committed to enhancing our assessment strategies by adopting more data-driven, adaptive, and forwardlooking approaches to ensure continuous relevance and impact.

Recognising the current power supply challenges faced by residential and industrial stakeholders, we launched the
Solar Home System (SHS) products for SMEs. This brings our total funding support to over NGN8.4 billion, surpassing the NGN7.9 billion disbursed since inception.
Business leadership and key stakeholders have prioritised climate risk due to its immediate and long-term impact on business stability.  Climate change directly affects portfolio performance, asset quality, and overall financial health, making it a key focus in our sustainability efforts.
Renewable Energy Efficiency-REE
We actively financed green projects which comprise renewable energy and waste management businesses (mostly recycling).
As a result, we are actively working to mitigate climate risk across our investment portfolio, the communities we serve, and our business operations, reinforcing our commitment to sustainable growth and responsible financial stewardship. This commitment is demonstrated through our initiatives in Renewable Energy Lending and Pollution/Waste
Control.
These efforts include conducting extensive on-si

FCMB GROUP PLC ANNUAL REPORT & ACCOUNTS 2024

Introduction

The origins of FCMB Group Plc date back to 1977 with the founding of City
Securities Limited (CSL) - a combined stockbroking and issuing house business) established by Otunba Michael
Olasubomi Balogun who transcended to eternal glory on the 18th day of May
2023.
More information can be found at:
The legacy of the founder of FCMB Group lives on through the institutions he built and inspired, the bankers and leaders he trained, the lives he touched and the values he exemplified. These serve not only as a foundation for which the Group and its stakeholders are eternally grateful, but as also a beacon of hope and a guiding light for every member of FCMB Group. Over the next few pages, we share a few excerpts from his messages in our prior annual reports.
The success of CSL in the Nigerian capital markets, prompted Otunba Balogun to establish First City
Merchant Bank Limited as the 1st independent merchant bank founded by a Nigerian. The bank has since expanded and evolved into a financial services group, with operating companies divided along four business groups: The Banking Group (First City Monument Bank
Limited ('The Bank'), FCMB Bank UK Limited and FCMB
Microfinance Limited); Consumer Finance (Credit Direct
Finance Company Limited); Investment Banking (FCMB
Capital Markets Limited and CSL Stockbrokers Limited);
and Investment Management (FCMB Pensions Limited,
FCMB Asset Management Limited and FCMB Trustees
Limited). Listed on the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) with the ticker symbol 'FCMB', FCMB Group Plc has
39.6 billion ordinary shares held by over 555,000 shareholders.

outlook.text:
Outlook
Going into 2025, our focus remains on accelerating our digital transformation, reinforcing our culture of excellence and extending the power of the Group in building a supportive ecosystem in fulfilment of our purpose.
With the collective support of our ecosystem, including our people, investors, regulators, customers and partners, we will remain  committed to carrying forward the vision of our
Founder, building an institution, nation and continent in which future generations can take pride. It is both our responsibility and privilege to contribute to this transformative journey, driving sustainable growth and innovation across the continent.
The IMF has projected global growth at 3.3 percent both in
2025 and 2026, below the historical (2000-19) average of
3.7 percent as an upward revision in the United States offsets the downward revisions in other major economies.
Global headline inflation is expected to decline to 4.2 percent in 2025 and to 3.5 percent in 2026.
In closing, I would like to thank all our stakeholders for their unwavering support throughout the year.
Domestically, growth prospects are likely to be slightly elevated with the implementation of effective stabilization measures which are expected to enable broad-based sectoral growth in the short term. Key drivers, such as foreign exchange stability will sustain operations in the manufacturing sector, while power supply and fuel pricing stability are anticipated to reduce business disruptions thereby boosting overall economic performance. The effective coordination of fiscal and monetary policy measures will also be pivotal in curbing inflation. Real GDP growth is projected to rise to 5.5 percent in 2025.
Thank you very much for your attention. God bless you all.
Ladi Balogun
Group Chief Executive
Group Chief Executive's Report
